10 Pearls for Clinical Practice in Disorders of Gut-Brain Interaction
  • Source: American Journal of Gastroenterology, Vol. XXX, DOI: 10.14309/ajg.0000000000004136
  • Topic: Clinical guidance for disorders of gut-brain interaction (DGBI)
  • Authors: Chang L., Staudacher H.M., Ballou S., Barbara G.
Overview of Disorders of Gut-Brain Interaction
  • DGBI are common, clinically important conditions
  • Characterized by chronic or recurrent GI symptoms related to altered gut-brain signaling
  • Include: IBS, chronic constipation, functional dyspepsia (FD)
  • Arise from multifactorial dysregulation of the gut-brain axis
Key Pathophysiological Features
  • Altered gastrointestinal motility
  • Visceral hypersensitivity
  • Mucosal immune activation
  • Microbiome composition changes
  • CNS processing alterations
Diagnostic Framework
  • Diagnosis primarily by clinical criteria
  • Rome criteria classify DGBI by GI anatomical region:
  • Esophageal
  • Gastroduodenal
  • Bowel
  • Anorectal
Pearl 1: Make a Positive Diagnosis, Not a Diagnosis of Exclusion
  • Use careful history, physical examination, symptom-based criteria, limited testing
  • Testing should be guided by age and alarm features
  • Order tests when likely to change management
  • Do not ignore structural disease
Practical Diagnostic Tools
  • Bristol Stool Form Scale (BSFS)
  • Symptom pictograms
  • Simple gut-brain diagrams
Benefits of Positive Diagnosis
  • Reassures patients
  • Validates symptoms
  • Reduces unnecessary testing
  • Creates earlier pathway to effective treatment
Symptom Duration Criteria (Clinical vs. Research)
  • Research criteria: symptom onset ≥6 months prior, active symptoms in previous 3 months
  • Clinical care: apply with flexibility when symptoms are typical and bothersome, alarm features absent
Pearl 2: DGBI Are Real Disorders with Multifactorial Biologic Mechanisms
  • Routine investigations typically negative in DGBI patients
  • Tests fail to capture key features: gut dysfunction and subtle microscopic changes
Patient Education Explanation
  • "Your gut and brain are constantly communicating"
  • "In IBS, this communication system can become overly sensitive or dysregulated"
  • Normal digestion or stimuli (meals, gas, distension, stress) may be experienced as more painful or bothersome
Gut-Brain Axis Components
  • Bidirectional communication between gut and brain
  • Symptoms may be driven by:
  • Central factors (CNS processing)
  • Peripheral factors (gut dysfunction)
  • These domains often interact
Key Clinical Framing
  • DGBI should not be framed as purely "psychological" or "psychosomatic"
  • Stress and psychological comorbidity can amplify symptoms but are rarely the sole cause
  • Framing as purely psychological undermines trust and increases frustration
Multifactorial Disruption Example
  • Fermentable carbohydrates --> alter microbiome composition and activity
  • Dysbiosis --> ↑ intestinal permeability, ↑ mucosal immune activation
  • This sensitizes afferent pathways conveying pain signals to CNS
Treatment Implications
  • Supports holistic, biopsychosocial approach
  • Treatment should be individualized to predominant mechanisms
  • Many patients require more than one intervention
Pearl 3: DGBI Can Overlap with Other Conditions
  • Overlap between GI regions is common and clinically important
  • Patients should be assessed for multiple DGBI
Common Overlapping Patterns
  • IBS with functional dyspepsia
  • Functional dyspepsia with functional chest pain
  • These may reflect shared pathophysiology rather than separate diseases
Extraintestinal Comorbidities (Common)
  • Psychiatric: anxiety, depression
  • Neurologic: migraine
  • Systemic: chronic fatigue, fibromyalgia
  • Other: pelvic pain disorders
Impact of Comorbidities
  • Associated with greater symptom burden
  • Linked to poorer quality of life
Central Sensitization Role
  • May link GI and extraintestinal symptoms
  • Contributes to: widespread pain, fatigue, sleep disturbance
Other Potential Mechanisms
  • Microbiome alterations
  • Immune activation
  • ↑ intestinal permeability
  • Roles remain incompletely defined
Management Approach
  • Careful phenotyping of GI and extraintestinal symptoms
  • Move beyond single-organ focus
  • Address shared drivers:
  • Sleep dysfunction
  • Stress-related arousal
  • Altered pain modulation
  • Psychological factors
Treatment Components for Overlap
  • Symptom-directed medications
  • Neuromodulators
  • Dietician-guided therapy
  • Brain-gut behavior therapies (BGBTs)
  • Pelvic floor therapy
  • Sleep optimization
  • Coordinated management of comorbid pain or mood disorders
Pearl 4: Avoid Repeated "Rule-Out" Testing After Appropriate Evaluation
  • Serial testing can:
  • ↑ symptom vigilance
  • Heighten health-related anxiety
  • Delay treatment
  • Repeated testing reinforces impression that alternative diagnosis was missed
Recommended Communication Approach
  • Explain what has been ruled out
  • State which diagnosis best fits clinical pattern
  • Identify which symptoms would prompt reconsideration
Example Clinician Statement

> "Your evaluation does not suggest cancer, inflammatory bowel disease, or celiac disease. Your symptoms fit IBS, a medical condition involving altered communication between the gut and brain that results in changes in gut function. We will monitor for specific warning signs, but the next step is to treat the mechanisms causing your symptoms."

Benefits of This Approach
  • Preserves diagnostic safety
  • Reduces uncertainty
  • Decreases unnecessary healthcare utilization
Pearl 5: Interpret "Abnormal" Adjunct Tests Cautiously
  • Diagnosis should be based on:
  • Careful history
  • Physical examination
  • Alarm features
  • Limited testing
  • Application of Rome criteria
When Additional Testing Is Appropriate
  • Reserved for patients with:
  • Severe symptoms
  • Refractory symptoms
  • Rapidly evolving symptoms
  • Atypical symptoms
  • Not for persistent symptoms alone
Tests Requiring Cautious Interpretation
  • Fecal calprotectin
  • Breath testing for SIBO
  • Carbohydrate malabsorption testing
  • Microbiome testing
  • Bile acid malabsorption testing in IBS-D
Key Principle
  • Mildly abnormal results should be interpreted in clinical context
  • Results should not override the overall clinical picture
Pearl 6: The Therapeutic Relationship Is an Effective Treatment
  • Patient-provider relationship is a core therapeutic tool in DGBI
Benefits of Positive Relationship
  • Better treatment adherence
  • Lower health care utilization
  • Improved quality of life
Negative Relationship Impact
  • Feeling dismissed or misunderstood --> symptom amplification, poorer outcomes
Effective Clinician Approach
  • Active listening
  • Validation of symptoms as real
  • Clear explanations of gut-brain mechanisms
Shared Decision Making Elements
  • Transparent treatment planning
  • Increased patient confidence and engagement
Defining Success Collaboratively
  • Frame with realistic expectations
  • Examples of success metrics:
  • Improved symptom control
  • Fewer flares
  • Faster recovery
  • Broader dietary tolerance
  • Improved daily functioning
Relationship Foundation Benefits
  • Reduces maladaptive illness behaviors
  • Creates foundation for other therapies to succeed
Pearl 7: Align Treatment Approach to Severity and Complexity
  • Treatment should match:
  • Symptom severity
  • Functional impairment
  • Psychosocial context
  • Prior treatment response
Mild Symptom Management
  • Education
  • Reassurance
  • Lifestyle guidance
  • Limited symptom-directed therapy
Moderate Symptom Management
  • Structured treatment plan often needed
  • May combine:
  • Dietary modification
  • Pharmacologic therapy
  • BGBTs
Severe/Refractory Symptom Management
  • Often benefit from integrated care
  • Consider earlier introduction to prevent symptom escalation
  • Reinforce adaptive self-management
Patients Requiring Special Attention
  • High health care utilization
  • Marked dietary restriction
  • Trauma history
  • Psychiatric comorbidity
  • Sleep disturbance
  • Disability
Individualization Framework
  • Use multidimensional clinical profile approach
  • Incorporate:
  • Diagnosis
  • Dominant symptoms
  • Physiologic contributors
  • Psychosocial modifiers
  • Illness impact
  • Patient preferences
Treatment Goals
  • Improvement in:
  • Symptoms
  • Control
  • Resilience
  • Functioning
  • Quality of life
Pearl 8: Diet Is Effective but a Restricted Diet May Pose Risks
  • Diet is a cornerstone of DGBI management
Evidence-Supported Approaches (IBS)
  • Low-FODMAP diet: strongest evidence
  • Simplified FODMAP approach: emerging
  • Mediterranean-style approaches: emerging
Functional Dyspepsia Dietary Guidance
  • Large or high-fat meals often worsen symptoms
  • Practical advice:
  • Small, frequent meals
  • Reduced-fat meals
  • Avoidance of individual triggers without excessive restriction
Patients Who Should Avoid Restrictive Diets
  • Screen before prescribing dietary restriction
  • Contraindications:
  • Significant recent weight loss
  • Limited diet variety
  • Body image concerns
  • Current or prior eating disorders
Alternative Approaches for At-Risk Patients
  • Dietician-guided liberalization
  • BGBT
  • Symptom-targeted pharmacologic treatment
Low FODMAP Diet: 3-Phase Approach

1. Elimination phase: time-limited trial (4–6 weeks)

2. Reintroduction phase: FODMAP subgroups reintroduced, symptoms monitored

3. Personalization phase: diet liberalized based on reintroduction results

Fiber Supplement: Psyllium
  • Dose: ≥10 g/day
  • Benefits:
  • Improves global symptoms in IBS
  • Improves abdominal pain in IBS
  • Improves stool frequency in chronic constipation
Alternative Fiber: Kiwifruit
  • Dose: 2–3 kiwifruit/day
  • Recommended for patients with constipation who cannot tolerate psyllium
Probiotic Supplements
  • Effects are strain-specific and heterogeneous
  • May improve IBS and constipation symptoms
  • Selection should be guided by predominant symptom
Pearl 9: Neuromodulators Are Not "Antidepressants for a Psychological Problem"
  • At low doses, these agents reduce overactive signaling between gut and brain
  • Goal: reduce GI symptoms
  • Not being used primarily to treat depression
  • Some may also improve sleep or mood
How to Explain to Patients
  • "These medications help calm down the overactive communication between your gut and brain"
  • Benefits may take 6–8 weeks
Medication Selection Based on Pharmacology
  • Choice guided by pharmacology and predominant symptoms
Tricyclic Antidepressants (TCAs)
  • Examples: amitriptyline, nortriptyline, desipramine
  • Best for: pain-predominant DGBI
  • Especially useful with:
  • Diarrhea
  • Insomnia
TCA Side Effects (More Common at Higher Doses or in Older Adults)
  • Sedation
  • Dry mouth
  • Constipation
  • Urinary retention
  • Tachycardia
Preferred TCAs When Minimizing Side Effects Priority
  • Nortriptyline
  • Desipramine
Serotonin-Norepinephrine Reuptake Inhibitors (SNRIs)
  • Example: duloxetine
  • Consider when:
  • Pain amplification present
  • Widespread pain
  • Mood symptoms coexist
SNRI Advantage
  • Generally have fewer side effects than TCAs
Mirtazapine
  • Benefits:
  • Functional dyspepsia (FD)
  • IBS-D
  • Particularly with:
  • Nausea
  • Weight loss
  • Anxiety/depression
  • Sleep disturbance
Tapering Consideration
  • After 6–12 months of good symptom control
  • Can consider gradual tapering
Pearl 10: Brain-Gut Behavior Therapies Are First-Line for Many, Not "Last Resort"
  • No longer viewed solely as treatments for refractory patients
  • Increasingly integrated into care earlier
  • Evidence-based treatments
Targeted Mechanisms
  • Visceral hypersensitivity
  • Autonomic dysregulation
  • GI-specific anxiety
Patients Who Benefit Most
  • Pain-predominant symptoms
  • Stress-sensitive symptom flares
  • High levels of:
  • Symptom-related worry
  • Avoidance behaviors
How to Introduce BGBTs Effectively
  • Frame as skills training to reduce symptoms
  • Not as treatment for stress or psychological problems
Example Patient Explanation

> "In DGBI, signaling between the gut and the brain can become overly sensitive. Behavioral therapies help retrain that signaling system to reduce the intensity and impact of symptoms."

Types of BGBTs
  • Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT)
  • Gut-directed hypnotherapy
Table: Diet and Supplement Overview for IBS
Low FODMAP Diet
  • Features: 3-phase approach (elimination, reintroduction, personalization)
  • Advantages:
  • Efficacious for up to 70% of individuals
  • Established mechanisms of action (improves patient buy-in)
  • Limitations:
  • Restrictive --> risk of nutritional inadequacy and over-restriction
  • More burdensome than other diets
  • Some specialist products may be required (higher cost)
  • Dietetic supervision recommended but availability limited
Simplified FODMAP Diet
  • Features: Restriction of limited FODMAP types or foods; 3-phase approach recommended
  • Advantages: Less restriction, lower burden
  • Limitations:
  • Limited testing of efficacy despite wide use
  • Low-level restriction may still cause challenges
BDA/NICE Diet
  • Features:
  • Regular meals
  • Moderate portion sizes
  • Limit fatty meals
  • Caffeine in moderation
  • Alcohol reduction
  • Fiber modification for altered bowel habits
  • Advantages:
  • Less restrictive than other approaches
  • Encourages healthy diet pattern
  • Can be implemented by non-dietician provider
  • Limitations:
  • Lack of direct evidence of benefit
Mediterranean Diet
  • Features:
  • Emphasis on wholegrains, fruits, vegetables, legumes, nuts
  • Extra-virgin olive oil as main fat source
  • Moderate fish intake, low red/processed meat
  • Limit processed high-fat high-sugar foods
  • Advantages:
  • Inclusive rather than restrictive
  • Several RCTs showing benefit for GI symptoms
  • Cardiometabolic benefits
  • Limitations:
  • Tolerability of higher fiber intake variable
  • Certain trigger foods may still require avoidance
  • Food cost for some items may be barrier
Fiber Supplements (Psyllium)
  • Features:
  • Most evidence for psyllium
  • Introduce at low dose, increase to 10–20 g/day over several weeks
  • Available as powders or capsules
  • Advantages: Easy to implement, low dietary impact, low cost, safe
  • Limitations: Tolerability variable
Probiotic Supplements
  • Features:
  • Live microorganisms in capsules or sachets
  • Strain-specific effects
  • Often trialed for 4–8 weeks
  • Advantages: Easy to implement, low dietary impact, safe
  • Limitations:
  • Effects are strain/species- and dose-specific
  • May require trials of alternative strains
  • Optimal formulation unclear
  • Some cost
Conclusion: Key Principles for Effective DGBI Care
  • Begin with positive diagnosis, clear explanation, multidisciplinary treatment plan
  • Match approach to:
  • Mechanisms of GI symptoms
  • Extraintestinal symptoms
  • Illness impact
Practical Strategies Summary
  • Reduce unnecessary testing
  • Improve patient engagement
  • Help patients achieve:
  • Better symptom control
  • Broader functioning
  • Improved quality of life
Figure Summary (Figure 5)
  • Illustrates integrated approach to DGBI management
  • Emphasizes patient-centered care model
